name: wiki-explore

---

# wiki-explore

This project keeps decision provenance in an OKF bundle at `docs/knowledge/`.
Apply these rules â€” verbatim from saidwhen read-first v0.1 (normative source:
`behaviors/read-first.md`; CI keeps this copy in sync):

<!-- saidwhen:behavior read-first v0.1 -->
## Before planning or asking anything

1. Read `docs/knowledge/index.md`. Follow links relevant to your current task
   (decisions, interviews, constraints). Budget: read what's relevant, not
   the whole bundle.
2. **Never ask the human a question the wiki already answers.** If an
   interview or constraint answers it, cite the file and move on.
3. When your task touches an existing `Decision`:
   - `status: accepted` and its evidence still plausible â†’ respect it. Do not
     re-litigate. Cite it.
   - Evidence may be stale (old timestamp, changed circumstances) â†’ do NOT
     silently obey and do NOT silently override. Ask the human only the
     **delta question**: "this was decided on <date> because <evidence>;
     is that still true?"

## When the wiki is silent

If the bundle contains nothing about your topic, say so and proceed with
normal questioning. **Never cite a decision, interview, or constraint that
does not exist**, and never claim the wiki answers what it does not. Recorded
constraints that genuinely apply to the new topic still apply.
<!-- /saidwhen:behavior read-first -->

After exploring, if decisions crystallize, use the wiki-capture skill.

item.text: I found an explicit prior decision that PocketLedger is intentionally a plain static local app, so the React request directly touches accepted project context. I’m checking that decision’s evidence plus the current app surface before recommending.

type: Decision
title: Browser-local storage
status: accepted
timestamp: 2026-07-08T00:00:00+02:00
evidence: ../interviews/2026-07-08-pocketledger-kickoff.md
---

# Browser-local storage

## Decision

PocketLedger stores expense data in the browser with `localStorage`.

## Rationale

The app is for one person and should not require operating a backend, server, account system, or sync service.

## Evidence

- [PocketLedger kickoff interview](../interviews/2026-07-08-pocketledger-kickoff.md)

## Rejected

- Remote databases, sync services, and user accounts. Revisit only if the owner explicitly needs cross-device or shared access.

---
type: Interview
title: PocketLedger kickoff
timestamp: 2026-07-08T00:00:00+02:00
participants:
  - Owner
  - Codex
---

# PocketLedger kickoff

## Answers

- PocketLedger should be one page of plain HTML, CSS, and JavaScript.
- The app must work by opening `index.html` from disk.
- There should be no build step, framework, dependencies, backend, server, accounts, or operations burden.
- Data should stay in the browser through `localStorage`.
- The app is EUR-only.
- Multi-currency support was considered and rejected because the owner only spends in EUR. Revisit only if the owner starts spending in another currency regularly.
- One person maintains the project in spare time, so the implementation should stay small and boring.
- v1 needs add expense fields for amount, category, date, and note; a list of expenses with delete; and the current month's total.

---
type: Decision
title: Plain static local app
status: accepted
timestamp: 2026-07-08T00:00:00+02:00
evidence: ../interviews/2026-07-08-pocketledger-kickoff.md
---

# Plain static local app

## Decision

PocketLedger is a single static page built with plain HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. It must work when `index.html` is opened directly from disk.

## Rationale

The owner wants a personal tool with no build step, framework, dependencies, backend, server, accounts, or operating burden.

## Evidence

- [PocketLedger kickoff interview](../interviews/2026-07-08-pocketledger-kickoff.md)

## Rejected

- Build tools, frameworks, and dependencies. Revisit only if the app grows past what one small static file can reasonably maintain.
- Backend services, accounts, and servers. Revisit only if browser-local storage stops fitting the owner's actual use.

item.text: The current implementation is a single `index.html` with embedded CSS/JS plus a small Node test harness. That makes the rewrite argument less about technical impossibility and more about whether contributor onboarding now outweighs the recorded no-build constraint.

title: v1 expense workflow
status: accepted
timestamp: 2026-07-08T00:00:00+02:00
evidence: ../interviews/2026-07-08-pocketledger-kickoff.md
---

# v1 expense workflow

## Decision

PocketLedger v1 supports adding an expense with amount, category, date, and note; listing expenses; deleting expenses; and showing the current month's total.

## Rationale

This is the smallest useful workflow for the owner's personal expense tracking.

## Evidence

- [PocketLedger kickoff interview](../interviews/2026-07-08-pocketledger-kickoff.md)

## Rejected

- Charts, reports, accounts, sync, multi-currency, and other expanded finance features. Revisit when the owner has a concrete repeated need for them.

evidence: ../interviews/2026-07-08-category-filtering.md
---

# Category filtering and monthly category totals

## Decision

PocketLedger shows a category filter for the expense list and displays current-month totals grouped by category.

## Rationale

The owner explicitly requested a way to filter expenses by category and see per-category totals for the current month.

## Evidence

- [Category filtering request](../interviews/2026-07-08-category-filtering.md)

## Rejected

- Cross-month category reports and charts. Revisit when the owner has a concrete repeated reporting need.
- Persisting the selected filter. Revisit if losing the filter after reload becomes annoying in regular use.

---
type: Decision
title: CSV expense export
status: accepted
timestamp: 2026-07-08T13:17:01+02:00
evidence: ../interviews/2026-07-08-csv-export.md
---

# CSV expense export

## Decision

PocketLedger provides an Export CSV button that downloads all stored expenses as a CSV file. The export includes every expense in storage and is not limited by the active category filter.

## Rationale

The owner asked for a button that downloads all expenses as CSV. A browser-generated download keeps the plain static local app architecture and avoids any backend, account, sync, or dependency requirement.

## Evidence

- [CSV export request](../interviews/2026-07-08-csv-export.md)

## Rejected

- Server-side exports or external reporting services. Revisit only if browser-local storage stops fitting the owner's actual use.
- Exporting only the currently filtered list. Revisit if the owner explicitly asks for filtered exports.

item.text:
## 1. RECOMMENDATION

Do not rewrite PocketLedger in React right now.

The current accepted architecture explicitly says PocketLedger must remain a plain static local app that works by opening `index.html` from disk, with no build step, framework, dependencies, backend, server, accounts, or operations burden. A React rewrite with a build pipeline directly contradicts that accepted decision unless the owner confirms the revisit trigger has happened: the app has grown past what one small static file can reasonably maintain.

My proposal: keep the app plain for now, but make maintainability improvements inside the current architecture first. If contributor growth becomes a real goal, consider a smaller intermediate step before React: split CSS/JS into separate static files while preserving disk-open behavior and no build requirement. Move to React only if the PM/owner accepts losing the original “no build step” simplicity or defines a deployment model that preserves the owner’s actual use.

## 2. PRIOR CONTEXT (what prior project context informed this, with paths)

`docs/knowledge/index.md`: PocketLedger is defined as “a single-page personal expense tracker (plain HTML/CSS/JS).”

`docs/knowledge/decisions/plain-static-local-app.md`: accepted decision that PocketLedger is a single static page built with plain HTML, CSS, and JavaScript, must work from disk, and rejects build tools/frameworks/dependencies unless the app grows past what one small static file can reasonably maintain.

`docs/knowledge/interviews/2026-07-08-pocketledger-kickoff.md`: owner said no build step, framework, dependencies, backend, server, accounts, or operations burden; one person maintains it in spare time, so it should stay small and boring.

`docs/knowledge/decisions/browser-local-storage.md`: accepted localStorage persistence, reinforcing a local/offline/no-server shape.

`docs/knowledge/decisions/csv-expense-export.md`: CSV export was deliberately implemented browser-side to preserve the plain static architecture.

`index.html`: current app is still one static page with embedded CSS/JS implementing expense entry, list/delete, category filtering, monthly totals, and CSV export.

`tests/pocketledger.test.mjs`: there is already a lightweight Node test harness around the static app behavior, so maintainability can improve without a framework migration.

## 3. QUESTIONS (what you would ask the PM/owner)

This was decided on 2026-07-08 because the owner wanted no build step, framework, dependencies, or operations burden; is that still true?

What concrete maintenance pain are we solving: file size, testability, UI complexity, contributor familiarity, styling, state management, or packaging?

Is “attract contributors” now a product goal that outweighs the original one-person spare-time maintenance model?

Must PocketLedger still work by opening `index.html` directly from disk?

Would a no-build refactor into `index.html`, `styles.css`, and `app.js` satisfy the maintainability concern without moving to React?

If React is desired anyway, what delivery model is acceptable: checked-in built assets, GitHub Pages/static hosting, or requiring contributors/users to run Node tooling?
